Notes: Note: Energy booms defined as 2002–08 (commodity supercycle, +272 percent), 2009–14 (post-global-financial-crisis oil rebound, +49 percent), and 2020–22 (pandemic rebound and war in Ukraine +227 percent). Narrow bars (right scale) show the median change in percentage points of GDP, computed not across all countries but only across energy importers whose deficits deteriorated and energy exporters whose deficits improved.

Title: Drivers of the Increase in Government Debt: Global and Poland
Subtitle: (as indicated)
Sources: Sources: IMF, World Economic Outlook database; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: Each panel decomposes the change in the debt-to-GDP ratio between 2025 and 2031 into its primary drivers: the cumulative primary deficit, components of the snowball effect (effective interest rate, real GDP growth, and deflator GDP growth), and stock-flow adjustments. The snowball effect refers to the automatic change in the debt-to-GDP ratio arising from the components of the interest rate–growth rate differential applied to the existing debt.

SlideHeading: Although the interest-growth differential remains favorable, the margin is too narrow given the persistent primary deficit and positive stock-flow adjustments
Title: Country Decomposition of the Increase in Global Government Debt
Subtitle: (Percent of global GDP)
Sources: Sources: IMF, World Economic Outlook database; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: In the figure, the contribution of each entity to global government debt depends on the projected change in its debt and its economic weight (using GDP in dollars). Positive values indicate contributions that increase the global debt ratio. A country may show a positive contribution even if its domestic debt ratio is falling if its weight in global GDP is projected to increase significantly (compositional effect). Data labels in the figure use International Organization for Standardization (ISO) country codes.

SlideHeading: The gap to debt-stabilizing settings deteriorated by more than 1 percentage point of GDP, largely reflecting spending commitments in major economies

Subtitle: (Percent of global GDP)
Sources: Sources: IMF, World Economic Outlook database; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The fiscal gap is defined as the primary balance minus the debt-stabilizing primary balance (a positive gap indicates that the primary balance is above the level needed to stabilize debt, excluding stock-flow adjustments). Positive bars indicate factors that increase the fiscal gap; negative bars indicate factors that reduce it. "Snowball" effects represent the contribution of changes in effective interest rates, real GDP growth, and the GDP deflator to the debt-stabilizing primary balance. Data labels use International Organization for Standardization (ISO) country codes.

Subtitle: (Percent of GDP unless otherwise noted)

SlideHeading: Emerging markets (excl. China): A window of favorable financing so far, but uneven and not to be taken for granted amid global fragilities
Title: Compression in Emerging Market Bond Index Spreads
Subtitle: (Basis points)
Sources: Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P.; EUROPACE AG/Haver Analytics; JPMorgan; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The figure decomposes the median compression in sovereign spreads in emerging markets using a hybrid machine-learning econometric framework. Key predictors are selected through machine-learning methods (lasso and random forest), and their effects are estimated using fixed-effects panel regressions. Country-level contributions are aggregated into global (green) and domestic (orange) components. DXY = Federal Reserve US Dollar Index; EMBIG = JPMorgan Global Bond Index—Emerging Market; NFCI = Federal Reserve Bank of Chicago National Financial Conditions Index.

Title: Global Public Debt-at-Risk
Subtitle: (Probability density of the three-year-ahead debt-to-GDP ratio forecast using data from 2025)
Sources: Sources: IMF, World Economic Outlook database; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The figure displays the probability density function for the three-year-ahead forecast of the debt-to-GDP ratio using data from 2025, estimated using panel quantile regressions on various political, economic, and financial variables. The global sample is composed of 49 countries accounting for more than 90 percent of global debt. Filled circles indicate the predicted 5th, 50th (median), and 95th percentiles of the debt-to-GDP ratio (Furceri and others 2025).

SlideHeading: Global public debt remains sensitive to shocks — current conditions such as the Middle East conflict or buoyant AI investment add to risks
Title: Change in Debt-at-Risk: Severe Scenario: Middle East War
Subtitle: (Percentage points of GDP)
Sources: Source: IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The figures show the change in three-year-ahead upside debt risks under two scenarios: (i) prolonged and intensified conflict in the Middle East, consistent with the severe scenario of the April 2026 World Economic Outlook, and (ii) an adverse shock to investment in artificial intelligence, assumed to increase the Index of Financial Stress (Ahir and others 2023) by one standard deviation in all economies. AEs = advanced economies; EMDEs = emerging market and developing economies.

SlideHeading: The convenience yield of US Treasuries is eroding, lifting the global risk-free benchmark and financing costs worldwide
Title: Convenience Yield of US Treasuries
Subtitle: (Basis points)
Sources: Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P.; Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, Federal Reserve Economic Data; LSEG Datastream; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The figure displays the option-adjusted spread between yields of AAA-rated US corporate bonds and US Treasury yields; a narrowing spread implies that the premium investors pay for the safety and liquidity of Treasuries (relative to high-grade corporate debt) is compressing.

SlideHeading: The convenience yield of US Treasuries is eroding, lifting the global risk-free benchmark and financing costs worldwide
Title: International Convenience Yield versus G10 Sovereign Bonds
Subtitle: (Basis points)
Sources: Sources: Bloomberg Finance L.P.; Federal Reserve Bank of St. Louis, Federal Reserve Economic Data; LSEG Datastream; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The international convenience yield measures the premium investors accept on US Treasuries relative to G10 sovereign bonds after adjusting for currency-hedging costs via covered interest parity. A higher value indicates greater relative demand for US Treasuries. The 10-year series includes the British pound, Canadian dollar, euro, Japanese yen, New Zealand dollar, Norwegian krone, Swiss franc, and Swedish krona; the three-month series includes the British pound, Canadian dollar, Danish krone, euro, Japanese yen, New Zealand dollar, Swiss franc, and Swedish krona.

SlideHeading: Short-term issuance offers interest savings but amplifies debt dynamics under stress, particularly at high debt levels
Title: Simulated Change in the Debt-to-GDP Ratio One Year After an Increase in the Cost of Short-Term Borrowing
Subtitle: (Percentage points of GDP)
Sources: Source: IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The figure displays a simulation from a New Keynesian model, featuring bonds-in-utility specifications of Alpanda and Kabaca (2020) and Mian, Straub, and Sufi (2025). The bond portfolio used in the simulation includes short-term bonds (one-year-maturity) and long-term bonds (five-year-maturity, on average). The shock in the simulation increases the short-term rate by 2 percentage points. A country’s debt portfolio is defined as short (long) maturity if 80 percent of the bonds in the portfolio are short-term (long-term) bonds.

SlideHeading: Global spillovers from US Treasury stress reach both financial markets and real activity
Title: International Spillovers from Shocks to the Supply of US Treasuries
Subtitle: (as indicated)
Sources: Sources: Smirnov 2026; and IMF staff calculations.
Notes: Note: The left panel illustrates the impulse response function for bond market spillovers, showing how much foreign 10-year yields change for every movement of 1 basis point in US 10-year yields over a 10-day horizon as a result of the Treasury supply shock. The right panel shows the impulse response of foreign industrial production (in cumulative log change) over a 24-month horizon to a shock that raises the US 10-year yield by 1 basis point on the day of the shock. Shocks are identified using high-frequency futures price movements around US Treasury auction windows (Phillot 2025) and are then refined to isolate the debt-supply news component of the announcement. Shocks cover the period from October 21, 1998, to January 23, 2020, and spillovers are computed using a sample of 66 economies.
